The program is an outgrowth of the effort begun over 20 years ago in the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion program to make a molten-salt reactor power plant for aircraft.
A molten-salt reactor - the Aircraft Reactor Experiment - was operated at ORNL in 1954 as part of the ANP program.
Fuel for this type of reactor would be $^{233}\mathrm{UF}_4$ dissolved in a salt that is a mixture of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , but it could be started up with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ or plutonium.
A major program activity through 1969 was the operation of the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment.
The MSRE operated at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and produced $7.3\mathrm{MW}$ of heat.
The initial fuel contained 0.9 mole $\% \mathrm{UF_4}$ , $5\% \mathrm{ZrF_4}$ , $29\% \mathrm{BeF_2}$ , and $65\%$ LiF; the uranium was about $33\%$ U.

The second phase of MSRE operation began in August 1968, when a small facility in the MSRE building was used to remove the original uranium charge from the fuel salt by treatment with gaseous $\mathbf{F}_2$ .
In six days of fluorination, $221~\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium was removed from the molten salt and loaded onto absorbers filled with sodium fluoride pellets.
After the fuel was processed, a charge of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was added to the original carrier salt, and in October 1968 the MSRE became the world's first reactor to operate on $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The nuclear characteristics of the MSRE with the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ were close to the predictions, and the reactor was quite stable.
In September 1969, small amounts of $\mathrm{PuF}_3$ were added to the fuel to obtain some experience with plutonium in a molten-salt reactor.
The MSRE was shut down permanently December 12, 1969, so that the funds supporting its operation could be used elsewhere in the research and development program.

The tertiary circulation loop, which transports heat from the secondary heat exchangers to the steam generators and reheaters, uses a commercially available nitrate-nitrite heat transfer salt, $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ - $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ with the eutectic composition 44.2-48.9-6.9 mole%.

In addition to being an effective chemical trap for tritium, the salt has a desirably low liquidus temperature of $288^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , a relatively low cost of about $15\phi/\mathrm{lb}$ , and good heat transfer and flow properties (see Table 3.1).
For example, the data available to date indicate that the salt would be compatible with Incoloy 800, a material that has proven acceptable for service in high-temperature steam systems.
The previous MSBR conceptual design employing sodium fluoroborate coolant salt to transport heat to the steam generators provided a minimum feedwater temperature of $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Although this minimum has not been established experimentally, the $725^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ liquidus temperature of the salt is likely to dictate feedwater temperatures well above those in conventional regenerative feedwater heating systems.

In the steam system flowsheet for the reference design MSBR, the cold reheat steam is preheated from 550 to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ by heat exchange with 3500-psia $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ steam taken from the throttle supply, and the feedwater is heated to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ by direct mixing with the exit heating steam from the reheat steam preheater.

The transport behavior of tritium in a 1000-MW(e) MSBR was examined previously, and various hypothetical methods for its control and containment were considered.
It was found that a bubble fraction in the range of 0.01 to 0.1 circulating in the salt streams had negligible effect on the tritium transport.

A purge gas flow sufficient to remove essentially all the tritium from a side stream of $10\%$ of the salt flow through the reactor reduced the amount of tritium reaching the steam to $0.2\%$ of the production.

With helium at high pressure and containing water vapor at a partial pressure of 1 atm, the tritium reaching the steam is negligible provided the water vapor is changed at a rate equal to or greater than $0.2\mathrm{lb / hr}$ .

If radiolysis were to increase the hydrogen concentration in the steam by a factor as great as 100, the water purge rate would have to be increased to $20\mathrm{lb / hr}$ to maintain the low tritium loss.
It would be desirable to keep the purge rate below about $1\mathrm{lb / hr}$ , but rates as high as 75 $\mathrm{lb / hr}$ might be acceptable.

If the water vapor partial pressure is as low as 6 or 7 torrs, water purge rates as low as $0.02\mathrm{lb / hr}$ are effective in preventing significant quantities of tritium from reaching the steam.
If radiolysis were to multiply the hydrogen concentration by a factor of 100, the purge rate would have to be increased to about $2\mathrm{lb / hr}$ .
A radiolysis factor as high as 100 is considered to be very unlikely.

A ratio of HF addition to HT diffusion into the coolant salt of at least 50 is required to hold the tritium reaching the steam to $1\%$ or less of that entering the coolant, the balance being carried off by the purge gas.
Increasing the purge gas flow from 20 to $100\mathrm{cm}$ , or by a factor of 5, has a negligible effect on the tritium transport, at least for large HF/HT addition ratios.
For all cases studied, a purge rate of 20 cfm established a residence time of 62.5 hr, and a rate of 100 cfm reduced the residence time to 12.5 hr.
For HF/HT = 50 the corresponding HF concentrations in the salt were 0.60 and 0.12 ppm.
For an assumed corrosion rate equal to half of the HF feed rate to the system and $\mathrm{HF / HT} = 5000$ , the calculated uniform rate of metal removal is about 0.001 in./year.

text: Proposals were solicited from a number of industrial firms to perform design studies of a 1000-MW(e) molten-salt breeder reactor.

The core is 45 in. in diameter and 57 in. high, with $15\%$ of the horizontal cross section open for salt flow.

The last set of calculations for the reference design (case CC-120), reported a year ago, was based on a reductive-extraction process which removed protactinium on a 3-day cycle, europium on a 500-day cycle, and other rare earths on a 50-day cycle; plutonium was assumed not to be removed, though in fact it probably would be removed (in a hydrofluorination step in the process).
The new set of calculations assumed that the fuel salt circulates through the processing plant on a 10-day cycle, with removal efficiencies and effective removal times that depend on the element.
In calculating the concentrations of noble gases and daughters, we assumed, as in the earlier calculation, that xenon and krypton are removed by gas stripping on a 50-sec cycle.
Also, as before, a fixed poison fraction of 0.005 was assumed to allow for absorption of $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ by the core graphite.

The performance of the 4-in.-ID gas separator was greatly improved by installation of the annular takeoff port in the recovery vane hub.

A straight cylindrical mixing section about 2.5 throat diameters in length was incorporated in the design to permit the bubbles to be formed and partially dispersed in a high-velocity region prior to reaching the diffuser.
The loop has been operated for short periods of time on water containing, separately, $n$ -butyl alcohol, sodium oleate, and $41.5\%$ glycerin.
The $n$ -butyl alcohol was used in concentrations up to $160~\mathrm{ppm}$ to study the effect of a surfactant that would not otherwise alter the properties of the liquid.

The sodium oleate was used in concentrations up to $40~\mathrm{ppm}$ with $4~\mathrm{ppm}$ of an antifoaming agent to determine the effect of reducing surface tension (from $72.5~\mathrm{dynes/cm}$ for demineralized water to about $45~\mathrm{dynes/cm}$ ).
The $41.5\%$ glycerin-water mixture is hydraulically similar to the fuel salt.

The addition of $n$ -butyl alcohol and sodium oleate may have caused a slight decrease in the size of bubbles produced by the bubble generator, but the most obvious effect was to stabilize small bubbles, inhibit their coalescence, and promote clouding of the loop fluid by bubbles 0.001 to 0.002 in. in diameter which were formed by the pump.
Operation with the $41.5\%$ glycerin-water mixture gave similar results except that the small bubbles were removed much more slowly.

Attempts to produce foams in molten salt by bubbling gas through a coarse glass frit produced bubbles 2 to $3\mathrm{mm}$ or larger in diameter under normal salt conditions, and a foam was generated only when moisture or hydrates were added to the salt.

The counter was an ionization chamber operated at $500\mathrm{V}$ with the output current read with a vibrating reed electrometer.
The uranium hydride-tritide trap could be heated to supply a pure dry mixture of $98\%$ $\mathbf{H}_2$ and $2\%$ HT to the convecting gas.
A side arm from the loop contained a Bourdon gage (vacuum to 15 psig) and a thermocouple gage.
The procedure involved first evacuating the nickel bar suspended in the heated quartz tube $(650^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ .
The temperature of the trap was adjusted to give the desired $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and HT concentrations and the gas allowed to circulate past the nickel bar for several hours.

The nickel bar was then placed in the quartz tube of the extraction apparatus in which the tritium was extracted from the nickel bar by heating it at red heat for several hours in a $99.9\%$ $\mathrm{He}-0.1\%$ $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ flow.
The gas then passed through a CuO trap at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to convert HT to HTO, and the HTO was finally caught in a series of distilled-water bubblers.
Before a gas was admitted to the loop, it had to contain less than 10 ppm of water as measured by a Meeco electrolytic water meter.

After once introducing a high tritium pressure $(\sim 100\mu)$ for a special test, it was not possible to get rid of a high background due to tritium dissolved in the thick stainless steel wall of the ionization chamber.

Analysis of the sampling devices for tritium is much simpler than analysis for hydrogen, since the nickel contains only about $0.02\mathrm{g}$ of $\mathbf{H}_2$ per gram and it is very difficult to avoid picking up small amounts of water from the surfaces of gas handling equipment.

For the nickel bar and nickel powder samples, a method for analyzing the extracted gas for $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ by mass spectrometer has been devised in case the tramp water problem turns out to be too difficult.

Spectrographic and chemical analyses are available on three samples, (1) the black lumpy material picked up from the pump bowl bottom, (2) the deposit on the latch stop at the top of the sample cage, and (3) material scraped from the inside of the mist shield in the liquid region.
The material recovered from the pump bowl bottom contained $7\%$ carbon, $31\%$ Hastelloy N metals, $3.4\%$ Be $(18\% \mathrm{BeF}_2)$ , and $6\%$ Li $(22\%$ LiF).

The hard deposit on the latch stop contained $28\%$ carbon, $2.0\%$ Be $(11\% \mathrm{BeF}_2)$ , $2.8\%$ Li $(10\% \mathrm{LiF})$ , and $12\%$ metals in approximate Hastelloy N proportions, again possibly to some extent cutting debris.

The sample taken from the inner liquid region of the mist shield contained $2.5\%$ Be $(13\% \mathrm{BeF}_2)$ $3.0\%$ Li $(11\% \mathrm{LiF})$ and $18\%$ metals (with somewhat more Cr and Fe than Hastelloy N); a carbon analysis was not obtained.
In each case, about 0.5 to $1\% \mathrm{Zr}$ ( $\sim 1$ to $2\% \mathrm{ZrF_4}$ ) was found, a level lower than fuel salt in proportion to the lithium and beryllium.

We first note that the major part of these deposits does not appear to be fuel salt, as evidenced by low values of $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ and $^{144}\mathrm{Ce}$ .
The values 0.13 and 0.11 for $^{144}\mathrm{Ce}$ average $12\%$ , and this is to be compared with the combined $24\%$ for $\mathrm{LiF} + \mathrm{BeF}_2$ determined spectrographically as noted above.
These would agree well if fuel salt had been occluded steadily as $24\%$ of a growing deposit throughout the operating history.

The noble metal fission products, 35-day $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ , 39.6-day $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ , 367-day $^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ , 2.7-year $^{125}\mathrm{Sb}$ , and 105-day $^{127m}\mathrm{Te}$ , were strongly present in essentially all samples.
In all cases 35-day $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ was present in quantities appreciably more than could have resulted from decay of $^{95}\mathrm{Zr}$ in the sample.
Antimony-125 appears to be strongly deposited in all regions, possibly more strongly in the upper (gas) region deposits.

Clearly $^{125}\mathrm{Sb}$ must be considered a noble metal fission product. $^{127m}\mathrm{Te}$ was also found, in strong concentration, frequently in similar proportion to the $^{125}\mathrm{Sb}$ of the sample.
The precursor of $^{127m}\mathrm{Te}$ is 3.9-day $^{127}\mathrm{Sb}$ .
The ruthenium isotopes were present in quantities comparable to those of $^{95}\mathrm{Nb}$ , $^{125}\mathrm{Sb}$ ; and $^{127m}\mathrm{Te}$ .

But if a delay or holdup occurred, then the shorter-lived $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ would be relatively richer in the holdup phase: the activity ratio $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}/^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ would exceed the inventory ratio, and material deposited after an appreciable holdup would have an activity ratio $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}/^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ which would be less than the inventory value.
Examination of Table 8.2 shows that in all samples, relatively less $^{103}\mathrm{Ru}$ was present, which indicates the deposits were accumulated after a holdup period.
Data for $2.1 \times 10^{5}$ -year $^{99}\mathrm{Tc}$ are available for one sample taken from the inner mist shield surface below the liquid level.

The value $1.11 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g/g}$ , vs inventory $24 \mu \mathrm{g/g}$ , shows enhanced concentration ratio similar to our other noble metal isotopes and clearly substantiates the view that this element is to be regarded as a noble metal fission product.
The consistency of the ratios to inventory suggests that the noble metals represent $\sim 5\%$ of the deposits.

If we take a median value of $\sim 300$ times inventory per gram for the deposited material, take pump bowl area in the gas region as $10,000\mathrm{cm}^2$ (minimum), and assume deposits $0.1\mathrm{mm}$ thick ( $\sim 0.02\mathrm{g/cm}^2$ ; higher values were noted), the deposit thus would have the equivalent of the content of $>60\mathrm{kg}$ of inventory salt.
There was $\sim 4300\mathrm{kg}$ of fuel salt, so on this basis deposits containing about $\sim 1.4\%$ or more of the noble metals were in the gas space.

The fact that gas and liquid samples obtained in capsules during operation had $^{103}\mathrm{Ru} / ^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ activity ratios higher than inventory and deposits discussed here had $^{103}\mathrm{Ru} / ^{106}\mathrm{Ru}$ activity ratios below inventory suggests that the activity in the capsule samples was from a held-up phase that in time was deposited on the surfaces which we examined here.

Experiments conducted by heating $\mathrm{NbF_4}$ in closed evacuated quartz tubes with one end at room temperature have suggested that the $\mathrm{NbF_4}$ disproportionates in the temperature range 250 to $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under conditions which maintain an $\mathrm{NbF_5}$ pressure of a few hundred microns.
The disproportionation of $\mathrm{NbF_4}$ at $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was found to produce a compound identified by x-ray diffraction as $\mathrm{NbF_3}$ (ASTM-9-168).
The $\mathrm{NbO_2F}$ decomposed above $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in a vacuum to form $\mathrm{NbOF_3}$ vapor, identified with a mass spectrometer, and a residue of niobium oxides.

The results of these spectroscopic studies indicate that an OH-containing impurity occurs in "pure" samples of $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ , that exchange with deuterium atoms takes place in the molten state, and that the OH species persists at melt temperatures of $425^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ over a period of days.

Additional experiments are under way to determine quantitatively the amount of OH-containing impurity in $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ and the extent of isotopic exchange of H for D or T in the molten state at temperatures of about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
